title: A Day in London
learningGoal: The learner will decode a multi-scenario narrative integrating polite transactional inquiries ('Excuse me', 'How much') and emergency imperatives, understanding the contextual triggers for register shifts.
instructions: Read the story of Raj arriving in London. He faces three survival situations: asking for directions, ordering food, and an emergency! Pay close attention to HOW he talks to people in each situation.

contentItems.text: Notice when Raj is polite (using 'Could' and 'Please') and when he is loud and direct.

readingText: Raj arrives at the airport in London. It is very big and he is lost. He walks up to a security guard. 'Excuse me, could you help me? Where is the train station?' he asks. The guard smiles and points to the left. 'Thank you so much,' Raj says. He takes the train into the city. He is very hungry, so he goes into a small restaurant. A waiter comes to his table. 'I would like the chicken sandwich, please,' Raj says politely. After he finishes eating, he asks the waiter, 'Can I have the bill, please?' He pays and leaves the restaurant. Suddenly, a man runs past him and takes his bag! Raj is shocked. He shouts loudly, 'Help me! Someone stole my bag! Call the police!' A woman hears him and takes out her phone.
textInTargetLanguage: Raj arrives at the airport in London. It is very big and he is lost. He walks up to a security guard. 'Excuse me, could you help me? Where is the train station?' he asks. The guard smiles and points to the left. 'Thank you so much,' Raj says. He takes the train into the city. He is very hungry, so he goes into a small restaurant. A waiter comes to his table. 'I would like the chicken sandwich, please,' Raj says politely. After he finishes eating, he asks the waiter, 'Can I have the bill, please?' He pays and leaves the restaurant. Suddenly, a man runs past him and takes his bag! Raj is shocked. He shouts loudly, 'Help me! Someone stole my bag! Call the police!' A woman hears him and takes out her phone.
baseLanguageSupportText: राज लंदन के हवाई अड्डे पर पहुँचता है। यह बहुत बड़ा है और वह खो गया है। वह एक सुरक्षा गार्ड के पास जाता है। 'माफ़ करें, क्या आप मेरी मदद कर सकते हैं? रेलवे स्टेशन कहाँ है?' वह पूछता है। गार्ड मुस्कुराता है और बाईं ओर इशारा करता है। 'आपका बहुत बहुत धन्यवाद,' राज कहता है। वह शहर में जाने के लिए ट्रेन लेता है। उसे बहुत भूख लगी है, इसलिए वह एक छोटे से रेस्तरां में जाता है। एक वेटर उसकी मेज पर आता है। राज विनम्रता से कहता है, 'मैं चिकन सैंडविच लेना पसंद करूँगा, कृपया।' खाना खत्म करने के बाद, वह वेटर से पूछता है, 'क्या मुझे बिल मिल सकता है, कृपया?' वह भुगतान करता है और रेस्तरां से निकल जाता है। अचानक, एक आदमी उसके पास से भागता है और उसका बैग ले जाता है! राज सदमे में है। वह जोर से चिल्लाता है, 'मेरी मदद करो! किसी ने मेरा बैग चुरा लिया! पुलिस को बुलाओ!' एक महिला उसे सुनती है और अपना फोन निकालती है।

glossary.baseTranslation: माफ़ करें (किसी का ध्यान खींचने के लिए)

exampleSentence.targetText: Excuse me, could you help me?
exampleSentence.transliteration: एक्सक्यूज़ मी, कुड यू हेल्प मी?
exampleSentence.baseTranslation: माफ़ करें, क्या आप मेरी मदद कर सकते हैं?

glossary.baseTranslation: मैं लेना पसंद करूँगा (I want का विनम्र रूप)

exampleSentence.targetText: I would like the chicken sandwich.
exampleSentence.transliteration: आई वुड लाइक द चिकन सैंडविच।
exampleSentence.baseTranslation: मैं चिकन सैंडविच लेना पसंद करूँगा।
किसी ने मेरा बैग चुरा लिया!
मेरी मदद करो! पुलिस को बुलाओ!

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: Can I have the bill, please?
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: कैन आई हैव द बिल, प्लीज़?
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: क्या मुझे बिल मिल सकता है, कृपया?

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: A man runs past him and takes his bag!
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: अ मैन रन्स पास्ट हिम एंड टेक्स हिज़ बैग!
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: एक आदमी उसके पास से भागता है और उसका बैग ले जाता है!

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: A woman hears him and takes out her phone.
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: अ वुमन हियर्स हिम एंड टेक्स आउट हर फोन।
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: एक महिला उसे सुनती है और अपना फोन निकालती है।

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: What does Raj say to the security guard BEFORE he asks his question?
Does Raj say 'I want the chicken sandwich' or 'I would like the chicken sandwich'?
When he is finished eating, what is the exact phrase he uses to ask for the check/receipt?
Why doesn't Raj say 'Excuse me, could you call the police, please?' when the man takes his bag?
options: Because he forgot the English word.
Because it is an emergency and politeness makes you too slow.
Because he is angry at the woman.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Fill in the blank from his emergency shout: '_____ me!'
What crime happened to Raj? (Someone _____ his bag).
According to the story, what does the woman do at the very end to help him?
True or False: Raj successfully changed his 'tone' depending on if he was paying a waiter or dealing with a thief.

correctAnswers.q3: Can I have the bill, please?
correctAnswers.q4: Because it is an emergency and politeness makes you too slow.

q3: Can I have the bill please

expectedSummaryPoints: Learner correctly identifies 'Excuse me' as the mandatory preamble for stranger interactions.
Learner grasps the register shift from polite interrogative ('Can I have') to urgent imperative ('Call the police!').

feedbackRules.onPass: Magnificent! You perfectly tracked Raj's journey! You noticed that he was polite to the guard and the waiter, but dropped the politeness immediately when his bag was stolen in the emergency.
feedbackRules.onFail: Keep trying! Remember, 'I want' is considered rude in a restaurant. Always choose 'I would like'. But in an emergency, you do not have time to be polite! Be direct and loud.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort! Pay close attention to Question 3. Asking for the check in a restaurant is an important survival skill: 'Can I have the bill, please?'

audioAssets.audioTranscript: Raj arrives at the airport in London... (full transcript provided in text)

hintText: Pay attention to the verbs. Raj uses 'Would like' and 'Could you' for the peaceful events, and 'Help' and 'Call' for the disaster.
commonMistakes: Thinking 'I want' is acceptable in formal service contexts due to direct Hindi translation.
